tickets.title: §21 — Schema identity: slice 2prime (Schema-as-entity + VersionedSchema); coexistence deferred
tickets.body:
SLICE 2 RE-DECOMPOSED (Myk agreed 2026-07-14) — the plan is now:
SLICE 2prime (BUILDING) = Schema as a first-class entity + per-version VersionedSchema snapshots, CO-LANDED (old slices 2+3 merged — the only §17-non-regressing unit). Lift the resolution Schema out of the registration to a LIVING schema:<name> entity (published via 0.5.0 SCHEMA_SCHEMA / publishSchemaClaims / loadSchema); the registration becomes a binding referencing it; §17's version door (readRegistrationVersions / resolvePinned / REST name@hash) resolves each old version against its FROZEN VersionedSchema snapshot (name@schemaCanonicalHex) — v1 keeps its old reading, exactly as today's inline freezing. SINGLE-LENS for now: Schema.name = hyperschema.name, entity schema:<hyperschema.name>. Ships ONE §20 migration (inline-schema registration → published Schema entity + binding + per-version snapshots; re-sign+negate; composes after slice 1). BREAKING → Myk's merge. See spec/21's clarified 'this snapshot IS §17's per-version freezing' paragraph.
SLICE 2b (DEFERRED — DESIGN-STAGE FIRST) = COEXISTENCE, two lenses over one hyperschema. spec/21 pins the registry key (hyperschema, schema-name) but the SERVING surface is UNSPECIFIED: gql.ts keys the type/query/mutations/hooks dispatch on hyperschema.name; rhizomatic's SchemaRegistry (frozen) refuses duplicate hyperschema names; and what the type is named when schema.name == hyperschema.name (today's universal single-lens case) is unaddressed. Needs a design pass (Myk) on the serving-surface model BEFORE build.
FUTURE (§23-driven) = VersionedHyperSchema (Film@termHash) — the symmetric hyperschema pin, so a fully-reproducible pin is (VersionedHyperSchema, VersionedSchema, roots). PURE LOAM, NO substrate change (termHash + HYPER_SCHEMA_SCHEMA already exist). Latent today; build when §23 needs the gather frozen.

---

SLICE 1 LANDED (2026-07-14, #92): the schema:→hyperschema: entity rename + the immutable-default flip (T1 wave B, now retired) + ONE §20 migration re-signing+negating both. schema: is now free for the Schema entities. REMAINING: slice 2 — the Schema as a first-class entity via SCHEMA_SCHEMA + publishSchemaClaims/loadSchema, named by Schema.name; registration demoted to a binding keyed on (hyperschema, schema-name) so two lenses coexist over one hyperschema. slice 3 — VersionedSchema = name@schemaCanonicalHex, coexisting/never-superseding, pinnable. Each is a PR for Myk (foundational).

---

UNBLOCKED (2026-07-14): the Schema Schema arrived — rhizomatic 0.5.0 ships `SCHEMA_SCHEMA` (#11), `Schema.name/alg`, and `schemaCanonicalHex`. §21 is no longer blocked on a decision; the 0.5.0 adoption LANDED (T8, #89), so §21 is now directly buildable, top of queue, against the real surface — and spec/21 was revalidated to cite the concrete 0.5.0 primitives (SCHEMA_SCHEMA, publishSchemaClaims/loadSchema for Schemas, Schema.name/alg, schemaCanonicalHex). Two of the old ambiguities are resolved by 0.5.0's shape: (1) the Schema Schema lives IN rhizomatic (parity, as filed in #11), not a Loam loam.schema.* fork; (2) the schema-name lives on the Schema itself (`Schema.name`). Remaining §21-native work is unchanged: the Schema as a first-class entity published via SCHEMA_SCHEMA; registration demoted to a binding keyed on (hyperschema, schema-name); VersionedSchema = `name@schemaCanonicalHex`, coexisting/never-superseding; Loam's `schema:`→`hyperschema:` ENTITY-prefix rename shipping its §20 migration; and T1 wave B (the immutable-default flip) folded into the same migration wave. 

---

BLOCKED (2026-07-13) — the §21 implementation hit a substrate wall (found by the build agent's safety valve; nothing was built, no guess shipped).

THE WALL: rhizomatic exports HYPER_SCHEMA_SCHEMA + publishSchemaClaims/loadSchema for the HYPERschema ONLY. There is NO Schema Schema, no at-rest delta vocabulary for the resolution Schema ({props, default}), and the Schema type carries no name — every `schemaName` in the code today is the HYPERschema’s .name. §21’s central move ("a Schema is a domain node with its own deltas, resolved by the Schema Schema, exactly as a Film is") presumes machinery that does not exist in frozen rhizomatic. Building it requires EITHER (a) rhizomatic grows a Schema Schema (a substrate PR + Myk’s call — the frozen-substrate hard limit, kin to the open rhizomatic#10 seam), OR (b) Loam invents a loam.schema.* vocabulary + a hyperschema-over-Schema-deltas. This is a (Myk) + rhizomatic-conversation decision; do NOT guess past it.

PROPOSED DECOMPOSITION (build agent, 2026-07-13): T2a `schema:`→`hyperschema:` prefix rename + §20 migration (most independently shippable; unblocks clean naming; breaking → Myk’s merge). T2b give the resolution Schema a name of its own (prereq for keying/snapshots; blocked on ambiguity 2). T2c key the binding on (hyperschema, schema-name), narrowing the duplicate-name refusal at gateway.ts:708 from "one lens per hyperschema" to "one living lens per (hyperschema, name)" [needs T2a+T2b; breaking]. T2d the Schema as a first-class entity [BLOCKED on the Schema Schema — ambiguity 1, the central wall]. T2e VersionedSchema name@hash snapshots, coexisting + genealogical (derivedFrom, never supersededBy) [needs T2d+T2b; reconcile with §17 registration-versions + the REST name@hash door at rest.ts:218,258]. T2f = T1 wave B: the immutable-default flip — flip assertWritable (gateway.ts:1218) so absent `writable` → nothing writable; every registration gains an explicit writable; migration re-signs old registration deltas adding writable=all-fields to preserve behavior; ALSO fixes a pre-existing bug (genesis.ts:63-68 builds registrationClaims without passing writable). Independent of the ladder but wave-coupled per Myk’s "one migration" note.
ORDER: T2a → {T2b, T2f} → T2c → T2d → T2e. T2d gates full T2c/T2e.

AMBIGUITIES NEEDING MYK (each forces an on-wire guess): (1) the Schema Schema — rhizomatic PR vs loam.schema.*? [gates everything]. (2) where the schema-name lives — on the Schema-at-rest (needs #1) or as Loam-local registration metadata? (3) new hyperschema prefix — recommend `hyperschema:<Name>` (shape-distinguishable); confirm. (4) VersionedSchema specifics — entity-id scheme, short-hash length (example @a1b2c3 = 6 hex — normative?), mint trigger (auto-per-publish vs explicit), the derivedFrom role name, and reconciliation with today’s §17 registration-delta versions + REST name@hash door. (5) wave fragmentation — may T2f (the flip) land its own migration first, or must it ride the final §21 PR? RECOMMENDATION: resolve #1 and #2 first; then T2a and T2f are each clean shippable PRs and T2c/T2d/T2e follow the spine.

--- ORIGINAL BODY (design landed in spec/21-schema-identity.md) BELOW ---

STATUS (2026-07-13): DESIGN LANDED — spec/21-schema-identity.md merged and accepted by Myk; every design question is answered. This ticket is now at the IMPLEMENTATION stage: build the code against the landed design (the design-stage prose below is the settled contract — implement to it, don't re-litigate it). IMPLEMENT: the Schema as a first-class entity with its own id/deltas; registration demoted to a binding keyed on (hyperschema, schema-name); VersionedSchema snapshots (name@short-content-hash) that COEXIST and are independently servable (never supersede — backwards-compat by construction); the schema:-prefix rename shipping its §20 migration (shape-distinguishable). This wave ALSO carries T1 wave B (the immutable-by-default flip) — one migration, one change, every existing registration gaining its explicit writable list. BREAKING on-wire → Myk's merge (P6), never self-merge.

---

DESIGN STAGE — the deliverable is a drafted SPEC §21 (staged as its future spec/21-schema-identity.md file, to land when the work merges) answering all six design questions, argued in a PR. Then STOP and wait for Myk's sign-off in chat before writing implementation code. 'He'd probably approve' is not his word. Lands BEFORE resolvers (§22/T3) and renderers (§23/T4): both need a schema identity that can be named, multiplied, and pinned, and today's registration model gives them none of the three.

WHAT THE PROBE FOUND (facts, with receipts):
- A second lens over a hyperschema REPLACES the first. A registration files under registration:<schemaEntity>, latest wins (registration.ts — registrationEntity), and the registry refuses duplicate hyperschema names, so hyperschema : Schema : roots binds 1:1:1 by construction. There is no way to hold two Schemas over one gather program — the thing the whole HyperSchema/Schema symmetry promises. Not good (Myk).
- The two halves have different standings at rest. The hyperschema definition is a REFERENCE (lives at its own entity, published as schema-schema deltas; the registration merely points at it). The Schema is a CARRIER (inline canonical JSON inside the registration delta). Half the decoupling road is paved; the Schema half never got its own identity.
- Naming conflation residue. The hyperschema definition entity defaults to schema:<Name> (schemaEntityFor), and Loam's prose says 'schema entity' throughout — for what is the HYPERSCHEMA. The 0.3.0 vocabulary pass expunged this at the delta level (rhizomatic.hyperschema.*) but not in Loam's ids and comments. (Rhizomatic's own loadSchema/publishSchemaClaims load and publish HyperSchemas — frozen substrate naming; a conversation in that repo, not ours to fix here.) Renaming Loam's schema: prefix is a breaking on-wire change → ships a §20 migration in the same PR.
- A proto-VersionedSchema already exists. readRegistrationVersions (§17) pins each surviving registration delta — Schema + roots as canonical JSON — by its content address, the version's TRUE NAME. What's missing is everything upstream of it: the version snapshots a REGISTRATION, because a registration is the only identity a Schema has.

THE PROPOSED LADDER (Myk): HyperSchema —many→ Schema —many→ VersionedSchema —many→ API (GraphQL, REST, whatever). A Schema is a LIVING domain node — a view computed from deltas via the Schema Schema, evolving like anything else — until it is snapshotted and REIFIED into a fixed VersionedSchema (plausibly literally the canonical JSON string, content-addressed). Doors serve VersionedSchemas; the living Schema goes on living.

DESIGN QUESTIONS:
1. DECIDED (Myk, 2026-07-12): YES. The Schema becomes a first-class entity — its own id, its own deltas, resolvable like any domain node — with registration demoted to a BINDING (this hyperschema + this schema + these roots, served here). The hyperschema half already works exactly this way; the design stage's job is the shape, not the whether.
2. DECIDED (Myk, 2026-07-12): a DISTINCT snapshot entity, not the registration delta — a version can exist, be named, and be pinned WITHOUT being served, exactly as the snapshot doctrine pulls (see T3). The design pins its shape (plausibly the canonical JSON, content-addressed) and its supersession wiring.
3. OPEN — many-to-many unlocking: the registration:<schemaEntity> keying and the name-unique registry both assume 1:1. A surface type then needs a name of its own, apart from the hyperschema's — what names a lens? (Argue in the draft.)
4. DECIDED (Myk, 2026-07-12): the binding/serving layer owns roots. Roots are a LIVENESS declaration (which entities stay hot), not a scope; they ride the registration-as-binding, never the Schema.
5. The naming pass — expunge the remaining schema/hyperschema blur in Loam's ids and prose; every on-wire rename ships its migration (§20).
6. OPEN — how the upper rungs stand on this: a resolver (§22) rides the Schema and is presumably part of what a VersionedSchema freezes; a renderer (§23) PINS a VersionedSchema — 'renderer pinned to schema vN keeps working forever' needs exactly this ladder under it. (Describe; §22/§23 depend on the answer.)

DEFINITION OF DONE (design stage): a drafted SPEC §21 answering all six questions, argued in a PR, and Myk's sign-off in chat — only then implementation.

CODE ANCHORS: schemaEntityFor (src/gateway/registration.ts:146), readRegistrationVersions (src/gateway/registration.ts:463), and registrationEntity + the duplicate-name refusal in the same file.

TRAPS: the schema: prefix rename (question 5) is on-wire — its migration rides the implementing PR, and the renamed entity ids must be shape-distinguishable from the old form; and rhizomatic's loadSchema/publishSchemaClaims names are frozen substrate — leave them, record the mismatch in prose only.

WAVE NOTE: the §21 implementing PR is the wave that also carries T1's immutable-by-default flip (one migration, one change). 'Lens' is prose, not a type today — question 3 is where the design decides whether it gets one; until then write Schema when you mean the Schema.

tickets.title: §22 — Custom resolvers: implement rung (a)
tickets.body:
REVALIDATION (rhizomatic 0.5.0, 2026-07-14): §22's claim that 'a resolver's content is part of what a VersionedSchema freezes' is now CONCRETELY BACKED — `schemaCanonicalHex` (0.5.0) hashes a Schema's `props` + `default`, so a resolver defined in a prop's Policy sits inside that hash: changing a resolver mints a new `name@hash` version, exactly as §22 requires, with no new machinery. v1 resolvers stay TEXT ESM (the 0.4.0 bytes kind is for §23's binary assets, NOT resolver source). One surface to inherit from T4: a resolver that RETURNS bytes would place a `BytesView` in the resolved view, which Loam's view serializers do not yet encode — see T4's BytesView note. Otherwise §22 is unaffected; it rides §21's now-concrete ladder (SCHEMA_SCHEMA + Schema.name + schemaCanonicalHex).

---

STATUS (2026-07-13): DESIGN LANDED — spec/22-resolvers.md merged and accepted by Myk; every design question is answered. This ticket is now at the IMPLEMENTATION stage: build the code against the landed design (the design-stage prose below is the settled contract — implement to it, don't re-litigate it). IMPLEMENT: rung (a) bucket-pure resolvers only — an optional resolve(deltas)→value downstream of the Policy; the rung AND the output type declared in the signed schema definition (so the doors generate correctly); memoize on (resolver-content-address, hash-of-selected-delta-set) with erasure invalidating by construction (a test proves a memoized value disappears when a delta it was computed over is erased). Describe the whole ladder, build only (a); synthetics (e) design-only. Rides §21 — do not start before §21 is implemented.

---

DESIGN STAGE — draft SPEC §22, argue it, STOP for Myk's sign-off before implementation. With questions 1–5 and 7 decided below, the design stage DESCRIBES the whole purity ladder, BUILDS rung (a) ONLY, and has exactly one open question left to argue: the caching/invalidation contract (question 6). Depends on §21 (T2) — rides its Schema ladder. Precedes §23 (T4).

THE PROPOSAL: today a field's Policy does two jobs in one breath — SELECTION (which claims count: pick's ordering, all's inclusion, conflicts' refusal) and REPRESENTATION (what value the survivors denote: a merge's fold, absentAs's rendering of silence). Factor out an optional Loam-level `resolve(deltas) → value`, downstream of the Policy. The Policy keeps the epistemics — whose claims, in what order, what counts as disagreement; the closed rhizomatic algebra, untouched and frozen. `resolve` overrides the semantics — what those surviving claims MEAN as a value in this lens. Absent a custom resolve, the Policy's built-in representation stands, exactly as today; nothing existing moves.

What it opens: a field whose value is a computation over its bucket (trend, histogram, latest-N), a field that consults siblings in the HyperView, a field that reaches past the selection into the store, even a field that leaves the store and asks a remote API. The View stops being limited to the six shapes the Policy algebra exports and becomes what an app means by its data — while the ground stays pure deltas and rhizomatic stays closed. Selection is trust-and-provenance work and MUST stay in the closed algebra (§14 leans on it — writes are the dual of resolution because resolution is a known program); representation was only ever a default, and Loam offers no way past it — that is the artificial constraint, and it is Loam-surface, not substrate.

THE §14 CONSONANCE: writes never needed the value function. assert/retract/clear/remove act on the BUCKET (the ground); clear works 'by construction' precisely because it lets resolution re-run without caring what resolution computes. So a custom resolve does not break the write mechanism; it breaks PREDICTABILITY (write x, read back f(x)). Loam already refuses to promise more (§13 — views are perspectival, absence is unknown); the surface just has to say so honestly.

THE PURITY LADDER (declared in the schema at rest, not discovered at runtime):
(a) bucket-pure — a function of the selected deltas only. Cacheable, deterministic, testable. The only rung v1 admits.
(b) hyperview-scoped — may read sibling fields' buckets. Deterministic given the HyperView; invalidation widens to the entity.
(c) store-querying — reaches past the selection. Deterministic given the store; hyperview-local invalidation is gone.
(d) effectful — remote APIs, clocks, anything. The view is no longer a function of the ground; two readers of the same deltas see different values. Admissible (lenses were never promised to agree) but forfeits caching, reproducibility, and any federation replay.
(e) synthetic — a Schema property with NO analog in the HyperView at all. No bucket, no Policy, no gather — resolve is the field's entire existence. A second axis, not just a higher rung: (a)–(d) grade what a resolver of an EXISTING field may READ; (e) drops the requirement that the field correspond to anything gathered — but it composes with the input rungs. Two consequences: a synthetic field is READ-ONLY BY DEFINITION (no ground to assert against — the §14-amendment derived-fields refusal arrives here, at the Loam layer), and it is where the overlap with rhizomatic's DerivedFn stops being naming and becomes design (question 2).

OPEN QUESTIONS:
1. DECIDED (Myk, 2026-07-12): OVERRIDE. resolve is optional atop an intact Policy, never a second resolution system beside it. Naming still needs care — no near-synonyms.
2. RESOLVED (Myk, 2026-07-12): read/write DUALS — keep both, touch nothing in rhizomatic. DerivedFn (substrate, implementations/ts/src/derivation.ts) is write-side: HView in, signed claims OUT — 'everything that computes is an author,' outputs on the record with rdb.derived.* provenance down to the input HView's content address. resolve is read-side: ground in, VALUE out, perspectival, never touching the record. The per-field choice is 'should this computation's output be a claim or an interpretation?' Rung (e) synthetics are resolve territory. VOCABULARY GUARD: 'derived' is reserved for the write side; the read side says 'resolver' / 'synthetic.' A rung (c)/(d) resolver that wants to REMEMBER what it computed doesn't want to be a resolver — it wants to be a derived author (§24's promote-the-outputs rides this dual).
3. DECIDED (Myk, 2026-07-12): v1 BUILDS rung (a) only; the design admits the whole ladder; the rung is part of the SIGNED schema definition, so a reader knows what kind of lens it is trusting.
4. RESOLVED (Myk, 2026-07-12): what is a resolver at rest = the SNAPSHOT DOCTRINE (transcribed below as a standalone subsection §22 owns; §23 inherits it verbatim). The residue is settled: EXECUTABLE SOURCE — the delta asserts directly-runnable ESM; what you audit IS what runs, one hash, no signed-vs-executed gap; building is the pusher's business, done before pushing; an optional provenance claim may link the runnable bytes to their pre-build sources for deeper audit.
5. DECIDED (Myk, 2026-07-12): writability stays orthogonal at every rung — writes hit the bucket, which is real; the surface documents the honest 'round-trip not guaranteed' posture for resolved fields. Rung (e) never was a question: no bucket, no write — refused with a reason.
6. OPEN (genuine design-stage work): the caching/invalidation contract per rung — what does the gateway promise, and where does memoization live?
7. DECIDED (Myk, 2026-07-12): YES. The resolver's content address is part of what a VersionedSchema freezes; changing a resolver is a new schema version under §17's append-only law.
8. OPEN (gap sweep) — output types for the doors: a resolver changes what a field's VALUE is, and the doors generate types from the registration (§17 — GraphQL fields, OpenAPI shapes). The signed definition must declare the resolver's output TYPE alongside its rung, or the surfaces cannot speak the field they serve. Without it, a resolver that turns a pick string into a histogram breaks every generated door silently.

--- THE SNAPSHOT DOCTRINE (DECIDED, Myk + Claude 2026-07-12) — code, schemas, and documents at rest. Cross-cutting doctrine, not its own section: it LANDS WITH §22 as a standalone subsection written for reuse; §21, §23, §24 cite it. Transcribe verbatim into the §22 draft. ---
Our deltas cannot do track changes — and neither can git's object model: blobs are whole snapshots, and every diff anyone ever read was computed at read time from two snapshots plus a lineage relation. That is not the missing feature; it is the design.
- Deltas assert VERSIONS OF COHERENT WHOLES, plus supersession claims relating them. Never edits. History is supersededBy lineage — §17's append-only law, close kin to §20's re-sign-and-negate. supersededBy STAYS Loam vocabulary, never substrate: negation is rhizomatic's because masking changes resolution's inputs; supersession changes nothing operationally — it is testimony readers follow, and lineage is a lens, per-reader. If cross-app interop wants it, standardize the role NAME in rhizomatic's docs, never semantics in its algebra.
- DIFF IS A LENS. Line diff, AST diff, semantic diff — derived views over two attested snapshots, chosen by the reader, never a storage format. Likewise the AST, outline, symbol table: structure WITHIN a unit is computed from the bytes, not stored as ground. The bytes are the ground truth; the AST never was.
- The granularity of a delta is the granularity of ATTESTATION — what would an author sign? Nobody signs a keystroke; nobody means an AST node in isolation. For code the unit is the module/artifact, and the signature attests EXACTLY the bytes that run (what §24's trust story requires). A swarm of signed fragments carries no signature on the combination a reader manufactures from them.
- Structure BETWEEN coherence units lives in deltas. Module entities with import edges, a document's outline — queryable, per-unit provenance, targeted supersession. The graph is delta-native; the leaves are atomic.
- ORDERING IS AN AUTHORED CLAIM. A container's arrangement is asserted as a value at the container level, signed as a whole; concurrent rearrangements CONFLICT visibly (§13) instead of interleaving silently.
- THE ECONOMICS LADDER: inline bytes → content-addressed ref → Merkle-chunked tree. All three are snapshot semantics; only storage costs change. Content addressing already dedups unchanged units across versions. v1 stops at the first rung that fits in a delta.
- Live collaborative editing, if it comes, is an EPHEMERAL LAYER (a CRDT session, an editor buffer) that periodically ASSERTS snapshots into the ground. Editing is conversation; the delta is the notarized statement at the end of it.
This is §21's picture in different clothes: a living Schema evolving as a domain node, reified by snapshot into a fixed content-addressed VersionedSchema. One picture, every rung of the ladder.

DEFINITION OF DONE (design stage): drafted SPEC §22 (with the snapshot-doctrine subsection) describing the full ladder, building rung (a) only, question 6 argued, Myk's sign-off in chat. Rung (e) is design-only in v1; describe synthetics fully and build none yet.

tickets.title: §23 — Renderers: push deltas, get software (DESIGN STAGE)
tickets.body:
REVALIDATION (rhizomatic 0.5.0, 2026-07-14): the bytes DEPENDENCY IS SATISFIED. §23's binary-asset story waited on the rhizomatic 0.4 `bytes` Target kind (rhizomatic#7); it shipped (0.4.0) and Loam ADOPTED it in T8 — a delta may now carry `{ kind: 'bytes', mime, value }`, riding federation's base64url transport transparently (Loam's target.kind checks are selective, so bytes fall through; see test/federation/bytes.test.ts). SURFACE IMPLICATION to design against (found in the 0.5.0 revalidation): rhizomatic 0.5.0's `View` type now includes `BytesView` (`View = Primitive | BytesView | …`), so a schema that resolves a bytes target INTO a view field yields raw bytes in the resolved VIEW — and Loam's view serializers (gql's `ViewValue` pass-through scalar in gql.ts, the REST node body) do NOT yet encode a BytesView to JSON (they pass a View through as-is; raw bytes are not JSON). BYTES-IN-VIEWS — DECIDED (Myk, 2026-07-14), the settled host-contract shape §23 builds to:
- A bytes value in a resolved view is a CONTENT-ADDRESSED REF by default, INLINE base64url for small values below a size threshold — the snapshot doctrine economics ladder (inline → ref → Merkle-chunked), chosen per-value by size. Ref-default because the bytes are already content-addressed (the ref is FREE), buying immutable cache-forever, dedup, small views, and native renderer ergonomics; inline saves a round-trip only when the value is tiny.
- SELF-DESCRIBING ENVELOPE: { mime, ref, base64url? }. `ref` (the content hash) is ALWAYS present — the stable identity, the /bytes fetch key, and the consumer cache key. The inline field is NAMED FOR ITS ENCODING: `base64url` — unpadded, url-safe, exactly rhizomatic's `b64uEncode`/`b64uDecode` and the same encoding the delta wire uses (NOT standard padded base64, which breaks a naive decoder) — present ONLY when inlined small, so the field name self-describes the decode. The discriminant is `base64url` presence, which tells the consumer BOTH inline-vs-ref AND how to decode; logic collapses to `data = v.base64url ? b64uDecode(v.base64url) : fetch(mount+'/bytes/'+v.ref); cacheKey = v.ref` — inline is a pure optimization, never a different object, and the content-address is always available to key on. A future second encoding is an ADDITIVE key (e.g. `hex?`), never a breaking change to the envelope.
- TWO LEVELS OF KNOWING: the field is TYPED as bytes by the door (this RIDES §22's resolver-output-type declaration — 'is it bytes' is a schema fact, so gql types it a BytesValue / OpenAPI format:binary); 'inline or ref' is the one value-level check.
- THE BYTE-DOOR: GET /:mount/bytes/<ref> returns the raw bytes with `mime` as Content-Type, inheriting §17 read discipline (serve only what the caller may LAWFULLY read — a raw-hash endpoint on the anonymous door would be the existence-oracle §17 already closed) and §11 erasure (a purged asset 404s).
- LATENT until a schema yields bytes, so Loam's view serializers (gql ViewValue, REST body) stay untouched until §23 implements exactly this. Depends on §22's output-type declaration and the bytes Target kind (adopted, T8).

---

DESIGN STAGE — draft the SPEC §23 section, then STOP for Myk before implementation code. Depends on §21 (T2 — a renderer pins a VersionedSchema) and §22 (T3 — the executable-delta / snapshot doctrine). See memory `renderer-task`.

THE VISION: a Loam store already carries its own schema, doors, and law — everything except its own face. Close that gap: a RENDERER is a UI component pushed as deltas, bound to a Schema and a route, and Loam ships a STOCK REACT HOST — a running app whose router is DERIVED FROM THE STORE. Push a renderer delta and the route exists; no build, no deploy, no app store between an idea and the people it's for. §17 said surfaces are materializations of the registration (GraphQL, REST, OpenAPI); this is the same law arriving at the screen: a renderer is a surface whose door is PIXELS. The database is the deployment.

WHAT BECOMES POSSIBLE: the village becomes a PLACE, not a script (a URL where every phase's stores render themselves, and growing a store mid-meeting grows the town in the browser); federation ships APPS, not just data (join a confluence and its interfaces arrive with its ground — a peer's board renders in your host, inert-by-default like foreign law §8/§12 until the operator blesses it); local-first, live by construction (a renderer subscribes to a View over local ground; writes go through the §14 verbs; offline is just the store being a store); an ecosystem on the substrate's own mechanics (renderers are signed, versioned §17, content-addressed, forkable, supersedable — the app-store problems of provenance/updates/rollback are already solved by the delta model, just pointed at UI).

WHAT MUST BE DESIGNED BEFORE ANY CODE (the real work):
- THE HOST CONTRACT — what a mounted renderer receives: a resolved View + a live subscription + the write verbs (assert/clear/remove) as CAPABILITY-SCOPED HANDLES, never raw store access. The renderer speaks lens; the host holds the keys. Pin this down FIRST — everything else is downstream. Two sub-questions (gap sweep): WHOSE PEN WRITES — a click in a foreign renderer becomes a delta signed by WHICH identity (the user's own key, or a per-renderer granted author so provenance shows the mediating code and revocation is per-renderer? §19's write-path labels — door, pen, wire, derived — gain a fifth value here); and THE MODULE CONTRACT — what the host provides ambient (React, the loam client) vs what a renderer must bundle INTO its snapshot unit. Ambient must be tiny and versioned: the bundle IS the attested artifact.
- THE ECONOMICS ARRIVE EARLY (gap sweep). A bundled UI riding in a delta is store-sized data: the browser peer's ~5 MB origin quota (§15) meets renderer snapshots immediately, so the snapshot doctrine's later rungs (content-addressed ref, chunked tree) likely graduate from 'later economics' to §23 v1 design questions. Budget for it; don't discover it in the demo. Related (Myk): a rhizomatic 0.4.0 `bytes` primitive — an ADDITIVE Target kind { kind: 'bytes', mime, value }, identity hashed over raw bytes (encoding is transport, never identity; base64url in JSON wire, native byte string in CBOR), MIME riding in-kind as attested interpretation-testimony. Additive ⇒ no migration (shape-distinct by construction, the §20 corollary free); old peers refusing the unknown kind is version discipline, not breakage. STATUS (Myk, 2026-07-12): rhizomatic 0.4.0 is IN PROGRESS and expected to land soon; it ships this bytes kind. BLOCKED-ON (external — not expressible as a ticket edge, which only relates Loam tickets): the STORE-SMALL-ASSETS-AS-DELTAS capability WAITS for rhizomatic 0.4.0 to land — do NOT design the inline-asset rung against a guessed shape, adopt the real 0.4.0 kind. The rest of §23 does NOT depend on it and can proceed in the meantime: the host contract, router discipline, trust/sandbox story, push-time verification, and versioning all stand on §21/§22, and v1 resolvers plus the renderer CODE bundle are text ESM — so only BINARY assets (images, fonts) need the new kind. Filed as rhizomatic#7 (https://github.com/bombadil-labs/rhizomatic/issues/7); §23's design NAMES it a dependency for binary assets and does NOT treat the primitive as a blob store (it is the inline rung only; big assets still climb the ladder).
- THE PUBLIC-DOOR TENSION (gap sweep). §17 deliberately serves only the LATEST version per declared name on the ANONYMOUS door (hash probes were an existence oracle) — but a renderer PINS a VersionedSchema, and village-as-a-URL wants strangers reading rendered routes. Reconcile explicitly: plausibly a public declaration may name pinned versions (a declaration is not a probe — the operator chose to reveal that version), but that is a §12/§17 amendment and must be ARGUED, not assumed.
- WHAT A RENDERER DELTA IS — RESOLVED by inheritance: the snapshot doctrine (see T3, landing in §22). A renderer at rest is a delta asserting the content-addressed bytes of a whole, versioned unit; the signature attests exactly what mounts; history is supersession. The source-vs-artifact residue is settled once, in §22's transcription — do not reopen it here.
- PROVEN AT PUSH TIME, NOT HOPED AT RUNTIME — a renderer declares the schema(s) + version it consumes; the door checks that declaration against the registered surface (the SurfaceGenerator seam, §17, is the natural anchor) and REFUSES a mismatch. Pin down the exact compatibility relation.
- VERSIONING UNDER §17 LAW — renderers born versioned, append-only; pinned to schema vN a renderer keeps working forever; decide what happens when vN is struck.
- TRUST FOR EXECUTABLE CONSUMERS — the sharpest edge. Who may push, whose renderers a host mounts, the sandbox story. Federation makes it acute; inert-by-default is the floor. The sandbox story does not start from zero: §6 already names object-capability confinement (SES / Worker / wasm compartments) as the discipline for federated code in the runner — renderers inherit that doctrine at the screen, they don't invent a parallel one.
- THE ROUTER DISCIPLINE — how a renderer claims a route, who owns the namespace, collisions, multiple renderers over one schema (a Schema is a lens; no reason it has only one face).

CLOSEST EXISTING MACHINERY: SPEC §17 — renderers are the read side of the same story; the SurfaceGenerator seam lives in src/surface/surface.ts.

REVIEW EXCEPTION: for review, use a SPECIALIZED PANEL (substrate-semantics · capability-security · correctness-API), not one generalist — the sanctioned exception to the one-reviewer budget rule (CLAUDE.md names capability/federation work as panel-worthy): three tightly-scoped angles, findings capped per angle, no separate verify stage (the fixer verifies while fixing — audit 1's retro). In ADLC terms this is the §23 P5 prosecution posture.

tickets.title: §24 — The quarantine: a place where untrusted law may bind (DESIGN STAGE)
tickets.body:
REVALIDATION (rhizomatic 0.5.0, 2026-07-14): no blocker change (still held for its dedicated session, after §23). Note only that SCHEMA_SCHEMA (0.5.0 — schemas as first-class publishable entities) tidies design-question 4 (promotion of law): promoting a quarantined schema OUT reuses the ordinary publish path (`publishSchemaClaims`/`loadSchema` over SCHEMA_SCHEMA), no special machinery. The erasure-into-quarantine test (question 8) and the one-way-glass are unaffected by 0.5.0.

---

DESIGN STAGE — draft the SPEC §24 section, then STOP for Myk before implementation code. The stretch goal of the arc. DO NOT OPEN THIS ITEM — even its design stage — before §23 (T4) is merged into the spec (spec/23-renderers.md): the host contract, the code-at-rest doctrine, and the trust UI are inputs here, and designing against guesses of them wastes the work twice.

THE GAP: today, foreign law is inert-by-default (§8/§12): a remote-authored schema, function, or renderer merges as data and binds nothing — safe, and also UNTESTABLE. The only way to see what a foreign lens does is to bless it, at which point it is already law. Close that gap with a QUARANTINE: a sandboxed environment where untrusted, remote-authored schemas, resolvers, and renderers actually RUN — bind, materialize, render — but where everything they produce is SEQUESTERED from the primary pool. Dry-run a stranger's whole app against your real ground, watch what it computes, and throw it all away if you don't like it.

THE SHAPE THAT WANTS TO EXIST: ONE-WAY GLASS. The quarantine reads the primary store's ground (or a snapshot of it) but writes only into its own pool; nothing crosses back without a deliberate act. That act is PROMOTION, in two strengths: (1) PROMOTE THE LAW — the operator blesses the schema/resolver/renderer itself into the primary store; it becomes bound like anything operator-registered. The quarantine was its probation. (2) PROMOTE THE OUTPUTS ONLY — the operator adopts specific deltas the quarantined thing produced (re-signed or endorsed as the operator's own claims) while the code that made them STAYS sequestered. 'I like what it said, not what it is.' And maybe this is not a feature but THE DEFAULT WORKFLOW: everything remote-authored lands in quarantine first, runs there, and blessing is always a promotion out of it — trust as a pipeline with a visible staging area, instead of a boolean flipped in the dark.

The snapshot doctrine (see T3) is load-bearing: the thing on probation is a fixed, content-addressed, author-attested artifact — not a swarm of fragments that might resolve differently tomorrow. Quarantine judges a snapshot; promotion promotes a snapshot.

DESIGN QUESTIONS:
1. DECIDED as posture (Myk, 2026-07-12): a SEPARATE STORE, federating one-way inbound. Stores are cheap, federation exists, discard = drop the store — and sequestration must not rest on every reader honoring a mark. The design stage PROVES it rather than debates it; if the proof fails, that is news — bring it back.
2. THE ONE-WAY GLASS, PRECISELY. Live-follow of the primary's ground vs a frozen snapshot; what 'reads but never writes back' means when the substrate is grow-only union. Honesty note (gap sweep): Loam has NO read-side capability slices today — the mount is the read boundary (§7) — so 'what quarantined code may see' is all-or-nothing unless this design invents something narrower. Don't reference machinery that doesn't exist; decide whether to build it or accept all-or-nothing.
3. PROMOTION SEMANTICS ON APPEND-ONLY GROUND. Promoting outputs cannot move deltas — it re-signs or endorses them (provenance preserved: adopted-from-quarantine, by whom, when). Pin the exact claim shape; close kin to §20's migration re-signing.
4. PROMOTION OF LAW IS REGISTRATION — does promoting a schema out of quarantine reuse the ordinary publish/register path (it should), and does the quarantine record survive as provenance on the blessed thing?
5. RESOURCE DISCIPLINE — quarantined code is the purity ladder's (§22) wild end running for real: caps on compute, lazy materializations, effectful resolvers calling out. The quarantine's budget must not degrade the primary store's doors.
6. THE WORKFLOW QUESTION — DECIDED (Myk, 2026-07-12): quarantine-first is the POSTURE for all federated law, with inert-by-default as its degenerate no-quarantine case. The default flips only when the quarantine actually ships; both remain expressible forever.
7. THE RENDERER TIE-IN (§23) — a quarantined renderer needs a host willing to mount it in a visibly-sequestered frame ('this is probation, its writes go nowhere') — the trust UI of the stock host.
8. ERASURE MUST REACH THE QUARANTINE (gap sweep). The quarantine holds a replica of primary ground; if tombstone + purge (§11) do not propagate into it, the quarantine becomes an erasure-EVASION vector — the forgotten bytes live on in the staging area, inside the operator's own walls. The one-way glass must carry the operator's tombstones IN even though nothing flows back out. Pin this with a TEST at design time; it is the §11 law arriving at §24, not an optional nicety.

DEFINITION OF DONE (design stage): a drafted SPEC §24 answering these questions (proving question 1's separate-store posture, and question 8's erasure propagation with a test), argued in a PR, Myk's sign-off in chat.

tickets.title: §23.8 — pinned-public: a public declaration may name a version
tickets.body:
§23.8 PINNED-PUBLIC — let a public declaration name a SPECIFIC version, so an anonymous door can serve a PINNED renderer route (or REST version). Buildable NOW: depends only on §23 v1 (LANDED #99) and §17's public door (LANDED). NON-BREAKING / ADDITIVE (a bare-name declaration is unchanged) → NO §20 migration. BUILD ticket, design settled below. Touches the anonymous read surface + a constitutional declaration → Myk's merge (P6).

WHY: §17 narrowed the anonymous door to the LATEST version per declared name, because an anonymous @hash probe was a registration-existence oracle. But a renderer PINS a VersionedSchema, and a public renderer route wants to serve that pinned version to strangers. DECIDED (Myk, 2026-07-15): a DECLARATION is publication, not a probe — when the operator NAMES a version in loam.public, they chose to reveal exactly that version, so the anonymous door may serve it. Every OTHER @hash stays a 404 (history stays un-probable).

DECIDED CONTRACT (spec/23-renderers.md §23.8):
1. VOCABULARY. A loam.public declaration's schema entries grow from ["Name"] to ["Name" | "Name@vN"]. A bare "Name" still means the LATEST version (unchanged). "Name@vN" means exactly that version, served anonymously because declared. Like renderers (§23.4), the vN is FROZEN at declaration time to the version's CONTENT ADDRESS (its registration deltaId, §17's true name) so the pin does not slide when an earlier version is withdrawn — store "Name@<deltaId>" in the declaration, resolving the operator's vN via registrationVersions at declare time.
2. THE DECLARE PATH. The gateway's declare-public path (where publicClaims is built/appended) resolves each "Name@vN" to "Name@<deltaId>" before signing (it has registrationVersions). publicDefect accepts both forms (a non-empty "Name" or "Name@<hash>"). readPublicSchemas returns a set that distinguishes latest-public names from pinned-public (name, deltaId) pairs.
3. THE DOORS. serveRoute's public branch (src/gateway/gateway.ts): a version-pinned renderer (binding.versionId) is served on the anonymous door IFF its schema+versionId is in the pinned-public set (today it is a flat 404 for any pinned renderer). Symmetrically, the REST public version door may serve /rest/@<deltaId> (and its vN alias) for a declared pinned version — reconcile with the existing 'public serves only latest' logic in src/surface/rest.ts (the withdrawn-vs-404 and latest-only rules). Everything not declared stays a uniform 404 to the stranger.

CODE ANCHORS (all on main): src/gateway/public.ts (publicClaims, publicDefect, readPublicSchemas — the whole declaration model), src/gateway/gateway.ts (the publish/declare path that appends publicClaims; serveRoute's `door === 'public' && binding.versionId !== undefined` refusal; surface('public')), src/surface/rest.ts (the public version-door discipline — latest-only, 410-vs-404), src/gateway/registration.ts (registrationVersions / RegistrationVersion.deltaId for the vN->deltaId freeze).

TRAPS: (1) FREEZE to deltaId at declare time, do not store the raw vN (the exact durability bug the renderers slice hit — §17 aliases shift on withdrawal). (2) keep the anonymous refusal UNIFORM (a declared-but-now-withdrawn version and a never-declared version must both look the same to a stranger — no oracle). (3) do not widen the FULL door's behavior (it already serves any registered version); this only widens the PUBLIC door, and only to DECLARED pins. (4) an actor without operator standing may not declare (same gate as any loam.public write).

RAILS / DoD (P3, tests first): (a) declare "Name@v1" public, anon GET the v1-pinned renderer route serves 200; (b) a pinned renderer whose version is NOT declared public is a 404 to the stranger; (c) a bare "Name" declaration still serves only the latest (a pinned route under it is 404 anon); (d) withdraw the declared version's registration -> the anon pinned route 404s (uniform, no oracle); (e) the operator (full) door serves the pinned route regardless of declaration. npm run check green. Update spec/23 §23.8 RECOMMENDATION -> DECIDED+built, JOURNAL, README ledger, and a village act showing an anon visitor loading a pinned public route.

tickets.title: §23.9 — renderer sandbox + timeout (worker_threads)
tickets.body:
§23.9 RENDERER SANDBOX + TIMEOUT — bound a renderer bundle's execution so a hanging or heavy bundle cannot wedge the process, on the anonymous door with an attacker-chosen entity. This closes the capability-security panel's HEADLINE residual on §23 v1 (#99): today serveRoute runs the bundle SYNCHRONOUSLY on the event loop with no timeout, so an infinite-loop bundle stalls EVERY mount. Buildable NOW (depends on §23 v1, LANDED). NON-BREAKING → no migration. BUILD ticket. Executable/capability surface → Myk's merge (P6); a capability-security review pass is warranted.

DECIDED (Myk, 2026-07-15): run each render in a Node WORKER (worker_threads) with a HARD TIMEOUT and resourceLimits. A Worker gives (i) a real timeout — terminate the worker if it overruns, which node:vm's timeout cannot guarantee against an async escape; (ii) memory bounds via resourceLimits; (iii) no shared mutable state by reference. NOTE the honest scope: worker_threads bounds the HANG / crash / wedge (the panel's actual finding) but is NOT full object-capability isolation on its own — a worker can still import fs / make network calls. True no-fs/no-net ocap (SES-in-worker or isolated-vm) is a FURTHER hardening, deferred to a deeper slice or §24; state this in code + spec, do not oversell.

CONTRACT:
1. serveRoute BECOMES ASYNC. Executing in a worker is async, so Gateway.serveRoute returns a Promise (and the byte-door / any render path awaits it). The HTTP `app` verb (src/server/http.ts) already runs in an async handler and already `await gateway.prepareRoute(...)` — add the await on serveRoute. Update every serveRoute caller (tests, the http handler) to await; keep the return shape { status, contentType, body }.
2. THE HARNESS. A worker script imports the bundle from its `data:` URL (reuse the §22.3 loader idea) and calls default(node); the main thread posts { bundle, node } and enforces a timeout (a tunable exported const, default 500ms). On overrun: worker.terminate() and return a UNIFORM refusal (500 'render timed out', no leak) — same clean-refusal discipline serveRoute already uses for a faulting bundle. On a thrown error inside the worker: 500 'the renderer faulted' (no leak). Prefer a SMALL WORKER POOL (spawn is ~ms; a per-request spawn is acceptable for v1 but note the cost) — each worker may cache imported bundles by content address. A function/closure cannot cross the thread boundary, so pass the bundle SOURCE + the node data, never loadedRenderer's fn.
3. resourceLimits (maxOldGenerationSizeMb etc.) bound memory so a bundle cannot OOM the host; pick conservative defaults as tunable consts.

CODE ANCHORS: src/gateway/gateway.ts (serveRoute — the sync try/catch render block becomes an awaited worker call; keep the read-discipline checks BEFORE the worker call), src/gateway/renderers.ts (loadRenderer / loadedRenderer — the main-thread ESM cache is now only for the sync fallback if any; the worker imports its own), src/server/http.ts (await serveRoute in both `app` cases), a new src/gateway/render-worker.(m)js worker entry + a pool helper, test/gateway/renderers.test.ts (make serveRoute calls await; add timeout + isolation tests). The §22.3 esm.ts loader is the model for importing ESM by content address inside the worker.

TRAPS: (1) the async ripple — every serveRoute caller must await; grep for serveRoute. (2) a Worker does NOT sandbox fs/net — document that clearly; this slice bounds the HANG, not ambient authority. (3) terminate() on timeout must not leak the worker (pool reclaim / respawn). (4) building/bundling a worker entry under tsconfig.build + the browser bundle scripts — mind that the worker file ships in dist. (5) keep serveRoute's read-discipline + proof-of-read (byte-door) checks OUTSIDE the worker (authorization stays on the main thread). (6) resolvers (§22) share the same in-process-exec residual — v1 T11 does RENDERERS only (the acute anon-door path); note resolvers can adopt the same harness in a follow-on.

RAILS / DoD (P3): (a) a bundle with `while(true){}` returns a 500 (render timed out) within ~the timeout and the process/other routes KEEP answering (prove the event loop is not wedged — a second route still serves during/after); (b) a normal bundle still renders 200 with correct HTML (the happy path unchanged, just async); (c) a bundle that throws returns 500 (faulted), no leak; (d) a bundle that tries to blow memory is bounded (resourceLimits) without killing the host. npm run check green (all serveRoute tests now await). Update spec/23 §23.9 (mechanism DECIDED=worker; the fs/net-ocap deferral stated), JOURNAL, README ledger.

tickets.title: §23.3 — write-enabled renderers (headless, granted-author pen)
tickets.body:
§23.3 WRITE-ENABLED RENDERERS (headless, granted-author) — a rendered route that can WRITE, not just read. v1 is the HEADLESS path: a server-rendered renderer paints a <form>, the form POSTs to a write endpoint, and the STORE signs the resulting delta under a PER-RENDERER GRANTED AUTHOR (§23.3's pen model). The user's-OWN-pen (non-custodial client signing) needs the browser host, so it is DEFERRED and edged after that slice. Depends on §23 v1 (LANDED). Additive → no migration. BUILD ticket. New write path + a pen-custody model → Myk's merge (P6); capability-security review warranted. SOFT-DEPENDS on §23.9 renderer sandbox (T11): a writing renderer should run bounded — note the interplay, not a hard edge.

WHY / THE PEN (DECIDED, §23.3): the write-path label is `renderer`. For an interactive renderer the click becomes a delta signed by WHICH key? Two answers: the USER's own pen (non-custodial — needs client-side signing, the browser host, DEFERRED) or a PER-RENDERER GRANTED AUTHOR (a distinct identity the operator grants write standing, revocable — provenance shows the mediating code). v1 builds the granted-author, SERVER-SIGNED path, which works headlessly.

THE CUSTODY MODEL (firmed 2026-07-15, mirrors §6's runner identity exactly — 'the operator blesses the recipe AND grants the runner identity write standing — the recipe and the key to the granary are different keys'): the operator PROVISIONS a renderer-pen identity by seeding it into the store's home/config (like an actor/runner seed the server can sign with), and GRANTS that identity write standing on the relevant entities (an ordinary grant delta, §7). The write endpoint signs form-submits with that seed, authored under the granted identity, labeled `renderer`. REVOCATION = strike the grant (the pen can then write nothing; past writes stay attributed). This reuses the existing actor-seed signing the server already does (contextFor(identity)?.actor in src/server/http.ts) — the renderer-pen is just another provisioned actor.

CONTRACT:
1. THE RENDERER BINDING gains (additively, in src/gateway/renderers.ts RendererSpec/binding) an optional `writable` (the fields this renderer's forms may write) and a `pen` (the granted-author identity name whose provisioned seed the server signs with). Absent → the renderer is READ-ONLY (today's v1). parseRendererInput + rendererBindingClaims + readRenderers carry them (mirror the `resolvers`/`writable` plumbing in registration.ts).
2. THE WRITE ENDPOINT: POST /:mount/app/<route>/<entity> (GET renders, POST writes) OR /:mount/app/<route>/<entity>/write. Body is form-urlencoded or JSON { field: value, ... }. The handler: resolve the renderer binding; refuse if it has no pen/writable (405/403); for each posted field, refuse a field not in the renderer's `writable` (400); compile to a §14 mutate via the SAME hooks.mutate the /rest and /graphql write doors use (surface(door).hooks.mutate), signing with the renderer-pen's provisioned actor seed (NOT the caller's token identity); re-render the route (or 303-redirect back to the GET) so a browser form submit lands on the updated page.
3. DISCIPLINE: the write runs the gateway's normal authorize/admission/tombstone path (the renderer-pen must actually HAVE write standing via its grant, else the write is refused exactly as any actor's would be). The anonymous door: a public renderer's form write is allowed ONLY if the operator both declared the lens public AND provisioned+granted the renderer-pen — otherwise 403 (no anonymous writes by default, §12).

CODE ANCHORS: src/gateway/renderers.ts (RendererSpec/RendererBinding/parse/claims/read — add writable+pen, mirror registration.ts's writable/resolvers), src/gateway/gateway.ts (serveRoute is read; add a writeRoute(route, entity, fields, door) that signs via the renderer-pen seed and calls hooks.mutate — model the pen-seed lookup on how options.seed / context.actor seeds are used in publishRegistration/mutateEntity), src/server/http.ts (the `app` verb: allow POST, parse the body, call writeRoute; the /rest POST handler in rest.ts is the model for body parsing + field validation + the mutate call), src/gateway/accounts.ts (grantClaims — how write standing is granted; the renderer-pen needs a grant), src/gateway/genesis.ts (how provisioned identities/seeds enter a store — the model for provisioning the renderer-pen seed).

TRAPS: (1) the server signs with the RENDERER-PEN seed, never the caller's token — the whole point is provenance shows the mediating code. (2) enforce `writable` at the door (a form field not in writable is refused) exactly as §14/§21 writability does. (3) the renderer-pen must have an actual GRANT to write the target entities, or the write is (correctly) refused — provisioning the seed is not authorization, the grant is (§6's two keys). (4) NO anonymous writes unless the operator explicitly provisioned+granted a public renderer-pen (§12 keeps every write gated). (5) where the renderer-pen SEED lives (home/config) is custody — document it; a compromised store home can sign as the renderer-pen (same trust as the operator seed already there). (6) form-urlencoded values are strings — coerce per the schema's field types like the REST write door does.

RAILS / DoD (P3): (a) a renderer with writable+pen: POST a field -> a delta is appended, authored by the RENDERER-PEN identity (assert the author), labeled renderer; (b) a POST to a field NOT in writable -> 400; (c) a renderer with no pen -> POST 405/403 (read-only); (d) the renderer-pen WITHOUT a grant -> the write is refused (provisioned != authorized); (e) revoke the grant (strike it) -> subsequent writes refused, past writes still attributed; (f) anonymous POST to a public renderer with no provisioned pen -> 403. npm run check green. Update spec/23 §23.3 (the granted-author server-signed path built; user-pen deferred to the browser host), JOURNAL, README ledger, and a village act: a rendered form that writes a guestbook entry under a revocable renderer-pen.
